pillar.metaDescription: VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation pays surviving spouses $1,699.36/mo tax-free in 2026. Learn eligibility & how to apply. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
pillar.h1: VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation: The Complete Survivor's Guide
pillar.quickAnswer: VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is a tax-free monthly benefit for surviving spouses, children, and parents of veterans who died from a service-connected condition. The 2026 base rate for a surviving spouse is $1,699.36 per month, effective December 1, 2025 (Source: VA.gov).
pillar.heroSubhead: Tax-free monthly support for the families of veterans who gave everything. Understand DIC, survivors pension, CHAMPVA, and how to claim what you're owed.
pillar.intro: If your spouse served and has passed, you may be entitled to tax-free monthly income and health coverage. This guide explains VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation and the survivor benefits that surround it.

sections.h2: What Is VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC)?
sections.body: VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is a tax-free monthly payment from the Department of Veterans Affairs to eligible survivors of service members and veterans. Unlike VA Survivors Pension, DIC is not based on your income or net worth: it is paid because the veteran's death was connected to their military service. The 2026 base rate for a surviving spouse is $1,699.36 per month, effective December 1, 2025, reflecting a 2.8% cost-of-living increase (Source: VA.gov). Survivors who qualify for additional provisions, such as having dependent children or needing Aid and Attendance, can receive more. DIC is one of the most valuable benefits available to a veteran's family. If you are unsure whether you qualify, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help you understand your options.

sections.h2: Who Qualifies for DIC as a Surviving Spouse?
sections.body: To receive DIC, you must be the surviving spouse of a veteran whose death resulted from a service-connected condition, OR a veteran who was rated totally disabled (100% schedular or TDIU) for a set period before death. You generally must have: married the veteran within 15 years of the discharge during which the qualifying injury or illness began or worsened; OR been married at least one year; OR had a child together. Remarriage can affect eligibility, though survivors who remarried on or after January 5, 2021 at age 55 or older may keep benefits (Source: VA.gov). Each situation is unique. Our licensed team at 1-800-MEDIGAP can walk you through the rules in plain English.

sections.h2: How Much Does DIC Pay in 2026?
sections.body: The 2026 surviving-spouse base rate is $1,699.36 per month, tax-free, effective December 1, 2025. Several add-ons may increase your payment: $360.85 per month if the veteran was rated totally disabled for at least 8 continuous years before death and you were married throughout that time; roughly $421 per month for each dependent child; a transitional $359 per month for the first two years if you have children under 18; and additional amounts for Aid and Attendance or Housebound status (Source: VA.gov). Because DIC is not means-tested, your other income does not reduce it. To estimate what your household may receive, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: DIC vs. Survivors Pension vs. CHAMPVA: How They Fit Together
sections.body: Survivors have three core VA programs. DIC is the income benefit tied to a service-connected death and is not income-limited. VA Survivors Pension is a needs-based payment for survivors of wartime veterans with limited income and net worth, capped by a Maximum Annual Pension Rate ($11,699/year for a spouse with no dependents in 2026). CHAMPVA is health insurance for survivors of veterans who died of a service-connected condition or who were rated permanently and totally disabled. You cannot receive both DIC and Survivors Pension at once; the VA pays the greater amount. Sorting out which combination is best for you is exactly what 1-800-MEDIGAP helps with.

sections.h2: How to Start Your DIC Claim
sections.body: You apply for DIC using VA Form 21P-534EZ, "Application for DIC, Survivors Pension, and/or Accrued Benefits." Submit it online at VA.gov, by mail, or with help from an accredited representative. Gather the veteran's death certificate, your marriage certificate, the veteran's DD-214, and any medical evidence linking the death to service. If the veteran died in the line of duty, the service department often files for you. Claims can be backdated to the date of death if filed within one year. Navigating VA paperwork is stressful during grief; call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll point you to the right resources and make sure nothing is missed.

faqs.q: Is VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation taxable?
faqs.a: No. DIC is a tax-free monthly benefit. You do not report it as income on your federal tax return, and it does not reduce based on your other earnings or assets because it is not a needs-based program. The 2026 surviving-spouse base rate is $1,699.36 per month (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How much is DIC for a surviving spouse in 2026?
faqs.a: The 2026 base DIC rate for a surviving spouse is $1,699.36 per month, effective December 1, 2025, after a 2.8% cost-of-living adjustment. Add-ons may apply for dependent children, the 8-year provision, and Aid and Attendance, which can raise your total monthly payment (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can I receive DIC if my spouse did not die in combat?
faqs.a: Yes. DIC is not limited to combat deaths. You may qualify if the veteran died from any service-connected condition, or if the veteran was rated totally disabled for a required period before death from any cause. Medical evidence linking the death to service is key (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Does remarriage end my DIC benefits?
faqs.a: Not always. If you remarried on or after January 5, 2021 at age 55 or older, or on or after December 16, 2003 at age 57 or older, you may keep DIC. Remarriage before those ages generally ends benefits, though eligibility can be restored if the later marriage ends (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can I get both DIC and Social Security survivor benefits?
faqs.a: Yes. DIC and Social Security are separate programs administered by different agencies. Receiving DIC does not reduce your Social Security survivor or retirement benefit, and Social Security does not reduce DIC. Many surviving spouses collect both simultaneously.

faqs.q: How long does a DIC claim take to process?
faqs.a: Processing times vary, often several months, depending on evidence and workload. Filing a complete claim with the death certificate, marriage certificate, DD-214, and medical evidence speeds review. Claims filed within one year of death can be paid retroactively to the date of death (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: What is VA Form 21P-534EZ?
faqs.a: VA Form 21P-534EZ is the application for DIC, Survivors Pension, and Accrued Benefits. One form lets the VA evaluate you for all three survivor programs. You can file it online at VA.gov, by mail, or with an accredited representative's help (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Where can I get free help applying for DIC?
faqs.a: Accredited Veterans Service Organizations and VA-accredited representatives help survivors file DIC claims at no cost. For plain-English guidance on DIC, Survivors Pension, CHAMPVA, and how Medicare fits with your coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and our team will point you in the right direction.

page.metaDescription: VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation pays surviving spouses $1,699.36/mo tax-free in 2026. See eligibility & rates. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.
page.quickAnswer: VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is a tax-free monthly payment to survivors of veterans who died from a service-connected condition. In 2026, the surviving-spouse base rate is $1,699.36 per month, effective December 1, 2025 after a 2.8% COLA (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: Tax-free monthly income for the spouses, children, and parents of veterans who died from service-connected causes.
page.intro: DIC is the VA's core income benefit for survivors of a service-connected death. Here's exactly how it works in 2026.

sections.h2: What DIC Covers and Who It's For
sections.body: Dependency and Indemnity Compensation is a tax-free monthly benefit paid to eligible surviving spouses, dependent children, and certain parents of a veteran or service member whose death was connected to military service. It is paid regardless of your income or net worth because it is compensation for the loss, not a needs-based program. In 2026, a surviving spouse's base rate is $1,699.36 per month (Source: VA.gov). DIC recognizes that a service-connected death changes a family's financial future. If you're navigating this for the first time, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for plain-English help.

The 2026 surviving-spouse base rate is $1,699.36 per month. You may add $360.85 if the veteran was totally disabled for at least 8 continuous years before death; about $421 per dependent child; a transitional $359 for the first two years when you have children under 18; and amounts for Aid and Attendance or Housebound status (Source: VA.gov). Because DIC is not means-tested, your wages, pension, or Social Security do not reduce it. To estimate your household's likely total, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help you understand the add-ons that apply.

DIC is an income benefit, but qualifying for it often signals you may also qualify for CHAMPVA health coverage and other survivor programs. Many surviving spouses are also Medicare-eligible, and coordinating Medicare with CHAMPVA or a Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan can close costly gaps. 1-800-MEDIGAP is the one trusted toll-free number for all things senior in America: call us to understand how DIC, Medicare, and supplemental coverage fit together so you're not paying for benefits you already have.

faqs.q: Is DIC the same as a VA pension?
faqs.a: No. DIC is tax-free compensation tied to a service-connected death and is not based on income. VA Survivors Pension is a separate needs-based benefit for survivors of wartime veterans with limited income and net worth. You cannot receive both at once; the VA pays the greater amount (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How much is DIC in 2026?
faqs.a: The 2026 base DIC rate for a surviving spouse is $1,699.36 per month, effective December 1, 2025. Add-ons for dependent children, the 8-year provision, and Aid and Attendance can increase the total monthly tax-free payment (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Do children qualify for DIC?
faqs.a: Yes. Dependent children may receive DIC, including added amounts when a surviving spouse is also receiving it, or a separate rate when there is no eligible surviving spouse. Eligibility generally extends to unmarried children under 18, or up to 23 if in school (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Is DIC paid for life?
faqs.a: For a surviving spouse, DIC generally continues for life unless you remarry before the qualifying age (55 or 57 depending on the date). If a disqualifying remarriage later ends, DIC eligibility can be restored. Children's DIC ends at the applicable age limit (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Who can help me understand my DIC benefits?
faqs.a: Accredited Veterans Service Organizations file DIC claims free of charge. For plain-English help understanding DIC alongside Medicare, CHAMPVA, and supplemental coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP. We'll make sure you understand every benefit your family is entitled to.

page.metaDescription: VA survivor benefits for widows include DIC, Survivors Pension & CHAMPVA. See 2026 rates and how to qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.
page.quickAnswer: VA survivor benefits for widows include three main programs: Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC), a $1,699.36/month tax-free benefit in 2026; needs-based Survivors Pension; and CHAMPVA health coverage. Eligibility depends on the veteran's service and cause of death (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: A clear map of the income, pension, and health benefits available to the widows of America's veterans.
page.intro: Losing a veteran spouse is hard enough without confusing paperwork. Here are the VA benefits widows should know about in 2026.

sections.h2: The Three Core VA Benefits for Widows
sections.body: Widows of veterans may qualify for three distinct programs. Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is tax-free monthly income of $1,699.36 in 2026 when the veteran died of a service-connected condition. VA Survivors Pension is a needs-based monthly benefit for widows of wartime veterans with limited income and assets. CHAMPVA is comprehensive health insurance for survivors of veterans who died of a service-connected disability or were rated permanently and totally disabled (Source: VA.gov). Each has different rules. To find which you qualify for, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free number for all things senior in America.

Your eligibility depends on the marriage and the veteran's service. For DIC, you generally must have married within 15 years of the qualifying discharge, been married at least one year, or had a child together. Survivors Pension requires the veteran to have served during a wartime period and your countable income to fall below the Maximum Annual Pension Rate ($11,699/year for a spouse with no dependents in 2026). Remarriage rules differ by program (Source: VA.gov). These rules overlap in confusing ways, which is why a quick call to 1-800-MEDIGAP can save you hours.

Most widows reach Medicare age, and VA survivor benefits do not replace Medicare. If you have CHAMPVA, you generally need Medicare Part A and Part B once eligible, and the two coordinate to cover more of your costs. Many widows still benefit from a Medicare Supplement plan to cover what's left. Getting this combination right protects both your health and your budget. 1-800-MEDIGAP specializes in helping seniors coordinate Medicare with VA coverage, all on one trusted toll-free line.

faqs.q: What benefits is a widow of a veteran entitled to?
faqs.a: A veteran's widow may qualify for DIC (tax-free income tied to a service-connected death), VA Survivors Pension (needs-based, for wartime veterans), CHAMPVA health coverage, burial benefits, and education assistance. Eligibility depends on the veteran's service and cause of death (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can a widow receive both DIC and a pension?
faqs.a: Not at the same time. A widow cannot collect both DIC and VA Survivors Pension simultaneously; the VA pays whichever is greater. DIC is usually the larger benefit and is not income-limited, while Survivors Pension is needs-based (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Do VA survivor benefits stop if a widow remarries?
faqs.a: It depends on the program and your age. DIC may continue if you remarried at age 55 or older (on or after January 5, 2021) or 57 or older (on or after December 16, 2003). CHAMPVA generally ends if you remarry before age 55. Rules vary, so confirm your situation (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How does a widow apply for VA benefits?
faqs.a: Widows apply using VA Form 21P-534EZ, which screens for DIC, Survivors Pension, and Accrued Benefits at once. File online at VA.gov, by mail, or with an accredited representative. Have the death certificate, marriage certificate, and DD-214 ready (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Where can a widow get free help with VA benefits?
faqs.a: Accredited Veterans Service Organizations assist widows at no cost. For plain-English help understanding VA survivor benefits alongside Medicare and supplemental coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free number for all things senior in America.

children.keyword: how to apply for va dic benefits

page.metaTitle: How to Apply for VA DIC Benefits | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Apply for VA DIC benefits with Form 21P-534EZ online, by mail, or with help. See the documents you need. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.
page.h1: How to Apply for VA DIC Benefits
page.quickAnswer: To apply for VA DIC benefits, file VA Form 21P-534EZ online at VA.gov, by mail, or through a VA-accredited representative. Submit the veteran's death certificate, your marriage certificate, and the DD-214. Claims filed within one year of death can be backdated to the date of death (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: A step-by-step walkthrough of the DIC application, the form you need, and the documents to gather.
page.intro: Applying for Dependency and Indemnity Compensation is straightforward once you know the steps. Here's how to do it right.

sections.body: The DIC application is VA Form 21P-534EZ, "Application for DIC, Survivors Pension, and/or Accrued Benefits." This single form screens you for all three survivor programs, so you don't need to apply separately. You can complete it online at VA.gov, download and mail it, or work through a VA-accredited representative or Veterans Service Organization who can file it for free. If the veteran died in the line of duty, the service casualty office often initiates the claim for you (Source: VA.gov). Not sure which boxes apply to you? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help you understand the form before you submit.

A complete claim is processed faster. Gather the veteran's death certificate showing cause of death, your marriage certificate, the veteran's separation papers (DD-214), and any medical records or a physician's statement linking the death to a service-connected condition. If you have dependent children, include their birth certificates. If you're claiming Aid and Attendance, add VA Form 21-2680 completed by a physician (Source: VA.gov). Missing documents are the top reason claims stall. A quick call to 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you build a checklist so nothing is overlooked.

sections.h2: Step 3: Submit and Track Your Claim
sections.body: Submit online for the fastest confirmation, by mail to the VA Pension Management Center, or in person at a VA regional office. After filing, you can track your claim status at VA.gov. Importantly, if you file within one year of the veteran's death, benefits can be paid retroactively to the date of death, so don't delay even if you're still gathering documents. Processing often takes several months (Source: VA.gov). For help understanding your decision letter and how DIC works with Medicare and CHAMPVA, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What form do I use to apply for DIC?
faqs.a: Use VA Form 21P-534EZ, the Application for DIC, Survivors Pension, and Accrued Benefits. One form evaluates you for all three survivor programs. File it online at VA.gov, by mail, or with a VA-accredited representative (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: What documents do I need to apply for DIC?
faqs.a: You'll need the veteran's death certificate, your marriage certificate, and the veteran's DD-214. Add medical evidence linking the death to service, children's birth certificates if applicable, and Form 21-2680 if claiming Aid and Attendance (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can I apply for DIC online?
faqs.a: Yes. You can complete and submit VA Form 21P-534EZ online at VA.gov, which gives the fastest confirmation and lets you track your claim. You may also mail the form or file through an accredited representative at no cost (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How long do I have to apply for DIC?
faqs.a: There is no strict deadline, but filing within one year of the veteran's death lets the VA pay benefits retroactively to the date of death. Filing later means benefits generally start from your claim date, so applying promptly protects your back pay (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can someone help me file my DIC claim for free?
faqs.a: Yes. VA-accredited representatives and Veterans Service Organizations file DIC claims at no charge. For plain-English help understanding the process and how DIC works with Medicare and CHAMPVA, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free number for all things senior.

page.metaTitle: DIC Benefits Eligibility for Surviving Spouse | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: See if you qualify for DIC as a surviving spouse: marriage rules, service-connected death & remarriage. 2026 guide. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.
page.h1: DIC Benefits Eligibility for a Surviving Spouse
page.quickAnswer: A surviving spouse qualifies for DIC if the veteran died from a service-connected condition (or was totally disabled for a required period before death) and the marriage met VA rules: married within 15 years of the qualifying discharge, married at least one year, or had a child together (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: The exact marriage, service, and remarriage rules that decide whether a surviving spouse can receive DIC.
page.intro: DIC eligibility comes down to two things: the veteran's death and your marriage. Here's how the VA evaluates both.

sections.body: The foundation of DIC eligibility is the cause of the veteran's death. You generally qualify if the veteran died from a VA service-connected condition, OR a condition shown by medical evidence to have caused or substantially contributed to death, OR the veteran was rated totally disabled (100% schedular or TDIU) and permanent for at least 10 continuous years immediately before death, regardless of the cause. Shorter total-disability periods can also qualify in specific situations, such as 8 years for an added rate (Source: VA.gov). Determining whether a death is service-connected can be complex; call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help you understand the path that fits.

To be a qualifying surviving spouse, you generally must meet one of these: you married the veteran within 15 years of the discharge during which the qualifying illness or injury began or worsened; you were married for at least one year; or you had a child with the veteran. You must also have lived continuously with the veteran until death (with limited exceptions, such as separations not your fault) (Source: VA.gov). These rules have nuances and exceptions. If your marriage doesn't fit neatly into one category, don't assume you're ineligible: call 1-800-MEDIGAP to talk it through.

Remarriage can change DIC eligibility. You may keep or restore DIC if you remarried on or after January 5, 2021 at age 55 or older, or on or after December 16, 2003 at age 57 or older. Remarriage before the applicable age generally suspends DIC, but if that later marriage ends by death, divorce, or annulment, you can apply to have DIC reinstated (Source: VA.gov). The dates and ages matter, and getting them wrong can cost you thousands. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand exactly where you stand.

faqs.q: Who is considered a surviving spouse for DIC?
faqs.a: A surviving spouse for DIC is someone validly married to the veteran who met VA marriage rules, lived continuously with the veteran until death (with limited exceptions), and has not remarried in a way that disqualifies them. Same-sex marriages are recognized (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Does the veteran's death have to be service-connected?
faqs.a: Usually yes, but not always. DIC is paid when death results from a service-connected condition. However, a surviving spouse may also qualify if the veteran was rated totally and permanently disabled for at least 10 continuous years before death, regardless of the cause of death (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: What if I was married less than a year?
faqs.a: You can still qualify if you married the veteran within 15 years of the qualifying discharge, or if you had a child together. The one-year rule is just one of three alternative ways to meet the marriage requirement (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can I get DIC if I remarried?
faqs.a: Possibly. DIC can continue if you remarried at age 55 or older on or after January 5, 2021, or at 57 or older on or after December 16, 2003. If you remarried earlier and that marriage has since ended, you may apply to have DIC restored (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How do I confirm whether I'm eligible for DIC?
faqs.a: The surest way is to file VA Form 21P-534EZ and let the VA evaluate your claim, ideally with help from an accredited representative. For free plain-English guidance before you file, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help you understand the rules that apply to you.

page.metaDescription: VA Survivors Pension helps widows of wartime veterans with limited income. 2026 MAPR is $11,699/yr. See if you qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: VA Survivors Pension for a Widow
page.quickAnswer: VA Survivors Pension is a needs-based, tax-free monthly benefit for widows of wartime veterans with limited income and net worth. From December 1, 2025 to November 30, 2026, the Maximum Annual Pension Rate is $11,699 for a surviving spouse with no dependents, and the net worth limit is $163,699 (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: Income support for widows of wartime veterans who have limited financial resources.
page.intro: Unlike DIC, Survivors Pension is based on financial need. Here's how it works for a widow in 2026.

sections.body: VA Survivors Pension is a tax-free, needs-based monthly benefit for the surviving spouse of a deceased wartime veteran. It is designed for widows with limited income and assets, and it does not require the veteran's death to be service-connected. Your payment equals the Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR) minus your countable annual income, paid monthly. For 2026, the MAPR for a surviving spouse with no dependents is $11,699 per year, or about $974 per month if you have no countable income (Source: VA.gov). If your income is low and the veteran served during wartime, this benefit may be for you. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find out.

Survivors Pension is means-tested. From December 1, 2025 through November 30, 2026, the net worth limit, which combines assets and annual income, is $163,699. Your countable income must fall below the applicable MAPR: $11,699 for a spouse with no dependents, $15,311 with a dependent child, $14,298 if you qualify for Housebound benefits, and $18,697 if you qualify for Aid and Attendance (Source: VA.gov). Many medical expenses can be deducted from countable income, which may help you qualify even if your gross income seems too high. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand how the math works.

sections.h2: Wartime Service and How to Apply
sections.body: The veteran generally must have served at least 90 days of active duty with at least one day during a covered wartime period (or 24 months for those who entered after September 7, 1980). Apply using VA Form 21P-534EZ, the same form used for DIC, so the VA can evaluate you for both and pay the greater benefit (Source: VA.gov). Because Survivors Pension and Aid and Attendance often work together for widows needing care, getting the application right matters. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for plain-English help before you file.

faqs.q: What is the income limit for VA Survivors Pension in 2026?
faqs.a: For 2026, a surviving spouse with no dependents qualifies if countable income is below the Maximum Annual Pension Rate of $11,699 per year. The combined net worth limit (assets plus income) is $163,699, effective December 1, 2025 (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Is Survivors Pension the same as DIC?
faqs.a: No. Survivors Pension is needs-based and for widows of wartime veterans with limited income, and the death need not be service-connected. DIC is not income-limited and requires a service-connected death. You cannot receive both; the VA pays the greater amount (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can I deduct medical expenses to qualify?
faqs.a: Yes. Unreimbursed medical expenses, including many insurance premiums and care costs, can be subtracted from your countable income. This often helps widows qualify even when gross income initially appears above the MAPR limit (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can a widow get Aid and Attendance with Survivors Pension?
faqs.a: Yes. A widow who needs help with daily activities may qualify for an enhanced MAPR of $18,697 in 2026 through the Aid and Attendance add-on, or $14,298 for Housebound status. You apply on the same pension application (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How do I apply for VA Survivors Pension?
faqs.a: File VA Form 21P-534EZ online at VA.gov, by mail, or with an accredited representative. The form screens for both Survivors Pension and DIC. For free help understanding which benefit fits your situation, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

children.keyword: va death benefits for veteran spouse

page.metaTitle: VA Death Benefits for a Veteran's Spouse | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: VA death benefits for a veteran's spouse include DIC, pension, burial allowances & CHAMPVA. See what you can claim in 2026. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: VA Death Benefits for a Veteran Spouse
page.quickAnswer: VA death benefits for a veteran's spouse include Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC), Survivors Pension, CHAMPVA health coverage, burial and plot allowances, and accrued benefits. DIC pays a surviving spouse $1,699.36 per month tax-free in 2026 (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: Every survivor benefit a veteran's spouse may be entitled to, from monthly income to burial support.
page.intro: When a veteran passes, several VA benefits may be available to the spouse. Here's the full picture for 2026.

sections.body: Two programs provide ongoing monthly income to a veteran's spouse. DIC is a tax-free benefit of $1,699.36 per month in 2026 when the veteran died of a service-connected condition or was totally disabled for a required period before death. VA Survivors Pension is a needs-based benefit for spouses of wartime veterans with limited income, with a 2026 MAPR of $11,699 per year (Source: VA.gov). You cannot receive both; the VA pays the larger amount. Figuring out which you qualify for is the first step, and 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you sort it out at no cost.

The VA offers burial benefits that can reimburse a spouse for funeral, burial, and transportation costs. Allowance amounts are higher when the death is service-connected and lower for non-service-connected deaths, with additional plot or interment allowances in some cases. Eligible veterans and their spouses may also be buried in a VA national cemetery at no cost, including the gravesite, opening and closing, headstone, and perpetual care (Source: VA.gov). These benefits ease a heavy burden during grief. For help understanding what your family can claim, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

A veteran's spouse may qualify for CHAMPVA, comprehensive health insurance for survivors of veterans who died of a service-connected disability or were rated permanently and totally disabled. Spouses may also claim accrued benefits, which are VA payments the veteran was owed but had not yet received at death. Coordinating CHAMPVA with Medicare and a Medicare Supplement plan can dramatically reduce out-of-pocket costs (Source: VA.gov). 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free number for all things senior: call us to make sure your health coverage works together, not against you.

faqs.q: What benefits does a veteran's spouse get when the veteran dies?
faqs.a: A surviving spouse may receive DIC or Survivors Pension (monthly income), CHAMPVA health coverage, burial and plot allowances, accrued benefits, and education assistance. Eligibility depends on the veteran's service and cause of death (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Does the VA pay for a veteran's funeral?
faqs.a: The VA offers burial allowances that can reimburse funeral, burial, and transportation costs, with higher amounts for service-connected deaths. Eligible veterans and spouses may also be buried in a VA national cemetery at no cost (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: What are accrued benefits?
faqs.a: Accrued benefits are VA payments the veteran had earned but not yet received at the time of death. A surviving spouse can claim these on VA Form 21P-534EZ, typically within one year of death, in addition to DIC or pension (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Can a spouse get VA health coverage after the veteran dies?
faqs.a: Yes, through CHAMPVA, if the veteran died of a service-connected disability or was rated permanently and totally disabled at death, and the spouse is not eligible for TRICARE. Medicare-eligible spouses generally need Parts A and B (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Where can I get help claiming VA death benefits?
faqs.a: VA-accredited representatives and Veterans Service Organizations help at no cost. For plain-English guidance on combining VA death benefits with Medicare and supplemental coverage, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free number for all things senior in America.

page.metaDescription: CHAMPVA gives a surviving spouse health coverage when a veteran died service-connected or was 100% P&T. See eligibility & Medicare rules. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: CHAMPVA Benefits for a Surviving Spouse
page.quickAnswer: CHAMPVA is health coverage for a surviving spouse when the veteran died of a service-connected disability or was rated permanently and totally disabled at death, and the spouse isn't eligible for TRICARE. Medicare-eligible spouses generally must have Part A and Part B to keep CHAMPVA (Source: VA.gov).
page.heroSubhead: Health insurance for the surviving spouses of veterans who died service-connected or were permanently and totally disabled.
page.intro: CHAMPVA helps cover a surviving spouse's medical costs. Here's who qualifies and how it works with Medicare in 2026.

sections.h2: Who Qualifies for CHAMPVA as a Surviving Spouse
sections.body: A surviving spouse qualifies for CHAMPVA if the veteran died of a VA-rated service-connected disability, OR was rated permanently and totally disabled (100% P&T) from a service-connected disability at the time of death, OR died on active duty (in which case TRICARE usually applies instead). You cannot have CHAMPVA if you are eligible for TRICARE. Remarriage before age 55 generally ends CHAMPVA, but it can be reinstated if that marriage later ends (Source: VA.gov). Determining whether the veteran's rating qualifies you can be confusing; call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help you understand your eligibility.

CHAMPVA coordinates with Medicare rather than replacing it. If you're entitled to Medicare, you generally must enroll in both Part A and Part B to keep CHAMPVA, and CHAMPVA then acts as a secondary payer after Medicare. This combination covers many costs, but gaps and cost-sharing can remain, which is where a Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan can help. Getting this coordination right is essential to avoid losing coverage or overpaying (Source: VA.gov). 1-800-MEDIGAP specializes in helping seniors fit CHAMPVA, Medicare, and Medigap together on one trusted toll-free line.

sections.h2: What CHAMPVA Covers and How to Enroll
sections.body: CHAMPVA covers a broad range of services, including doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, mental health, and many preventive services, with cost-sharing after an annual deductible. To enroll, submit VA Form 10-10d with supporting documents such as the veteran's death certificate, your marriage certificate, and Medicare cards if applicable, to the VA Health Administration Center (Source: VA.gov). Because CHAMPVA, Medicare, and supplemental coverage interact, a quick call to 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you enroll correctly and confirm you're not paying for overlapping coverage.

faqs.q: Who is eligible for CHAMPVA as a surviving spouse?
faqs.a: A surviving spouse qualifies if the veteran died of a service-connected disability or was rated permanently and totally disabled (100% P&T) at death, and the spouse is not eligible for TRICARE. Remarriage before age 55 generally ends eligibility (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Do I need Medicare to keep CHAMPVA?
faqs.a: If you are entitled to Medicare, you generally must enroll in Part A and Part B to remain eligible for CHAMPVA. CHAMPVA then pays secondary to Medicare, helping cover costs Medicare leaves behind (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Does CHAMPVA cover prescriptions?
faqs.a: Yes. CHAMPVA covers prescription medications, along with doctor visits, hospital care, mental health services, and many preventive services. Cost-sharing applies after an annual deductible, and a Medicare drug plan may also coordinate for Medicare-eligible spouses (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: How do I apply for CHAMPVA?
faqs.a: Submit VA Form 10-10d to the VA Health Administration Center with the veteran's death certificate, your marriage certificate, and Medicare cards if you have them. Processing can take several weeks (Source: VA.gov).

faqs.q: Should I add a Medigap plan if I have CHAMPVA and Medicare?
faqs.a: It depends on your costs and care needs. CHAMPVA pays secondary to Medicare, but gaps can remain. Many surviving spouses benefit from reviewing their options. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see whether a Medicare Supplement plan makes sense for you.
